text: Four incisions are made between the eighth and ninth right intercostal spaces for robotic trochars. The surgeon uses the da Vinci XI robotic system to approach the hilum from anterior to posterior, employing a fissureless technique.

The surgeon incises the mediastinal pleura and identifies the right upper pulmonary vein as the most anterior structure in the hilum. Using a combination of blunt dissection and electrocautery with a fenestrated bipolar forceps in the left hand and a marrow gland in the right hand, the vein is isolated. Care is taken to identify and preserve the middle lobe vein, which typically drains into the right superior pulmonary vein.

The surgeon pulls the lobe inferiorly to expose the main pulmonary artery, isolates and removes a small hilar lymph node between the pulmonary artery and vein. The pleura overlying the pulmonary artery is dissected, revealing the artery lateral to the superior vena cava. The right upper pulmonary vein is divided using a 30mm robotic vascular stapler, enhancing visualization of the pulmonary artery branches.

The surgeon lifts the stump of the vein and dissects the adventitial plane of the pulmonary artery, exposing the Boyden branch running parallel to the pulmonary vein's upper margin. A lymphadenectomy of Heiler station X is performed, aiding in the dissection of the Boyden branch on its lower margin.

The surgeon completes the isolation of the pulmonary artery and divides it using a 30mm robotic vascular stapler reload. The lung is then pulled anteriorly and inferiorly to access the bronchial structures. Electrocautery is used to dissect the posterior pleural reflection, exposing the right upper lobe bronchus, which is separated from the lower margin of the azygos vein with gentle blunt dissection.

The surgeon clears lung tissue from the angle between the bronchus intermedius and the upper lobe bronchus, exposing the posterior hilar lymph nodes. The right upper lobe is pulled posteriorly to facilitate this exposure.

The surgeon performs a precise hilar lymphadenectomy, enabling dissection of the lower margin of the bronchus, which is transected using a robotic stapler with a 45mm green reload. The fissures between the upper, middle, and lower lobes are clearly defined, and the division is executed with multiple parenchymal reloads.

The surgeon begins a station seven lymphadenectomy by opening the posterior pleura from the superior edge of the lower lobe vein, following the course of the vagus nerve, which provides a clear view of the esophagus.

The surgeon approaches the paratracheal area by dissecting the mediastinal pleura above the azygos vein and along the lateral profile of the superior vena cava. Lymph nodes from stations IV-R and II-R are removed en bloc.
The surgeon completes the procedure by extracting the lobe from the chest using a specimen retrieval bag through a utility incision created by joining the two middle robotic incisions.
